Hi Everyone -
I thought I would contribute to the collective knowledge here with my journey busting using Rivea Corymbosa Seeds, AKA "RC Seeds", AKA Ololiuqui. I will keep a log of it all in the format I've shown below. First I will give some quick background. (To skip to just the busting log, scroll to the bottom).
I'm a 35 year old male, first started getting CH when I was sixteen, in 2001. Cycles are generally ~7 weeks, and have been getting further and further apart the older I get, with ~3 years between cycles. When I was a teen I did the pharma route, finding mixed results with Prednisone taper and Inderal. But once those stopped working, a neurologist with a bad attitude started trying other drugs on me, and I started reading about the experiences of other CHers on clusterheadaches.com and here on clusterbusters, I said "no thank you" to the pharma route.
2003 - 2010
Sloppily experimenting with busting, different vitamins, prednisone/Inderal etc. I was 18-25 during this time, and you know how people are at that age! I had mixed results with mushrooms, and also had very mixed results with the high! One of the most enjoyable nights in my entire life was on mushrooms, and it stopped the CH dead in its tracks. I also had some of the most intensely anxious times in my life, and it didn't stop the CH. I attempted RC Seeds during this time, but back then the recommended starting dose was very low. I think I started with 8 seeds! So, I found no impact from using them.
2010 - 2020
Started the Vitamin D3 regimen. The D3 brought the severity of the pain during attacks WAY down. I've never been able to fully control a cycle using just the D3 regimen, but it has helped tremendously. During this time I also started using oxygen - *properly*! I was prescribed oxygen as a teenager, but was using the wrong mask and didn't have enough air flow, so I concluded that it didn't work for me. This was a total game-changer, obviously. I also started realizing that when I eat, I get attacks. I started talking to Batch a lot during this time. We talked about the body's PH and how keeping the body more alkaline could help. So I started drinking more lemon water, making tweaks like that, that I think helped a little bit. I tried 5-MEO-DALT for two cycles during this time as well. The results were mixed and I did not like the idea of weighing out these tiny, tiny increments of a random chemical I purchased in a sketchy way on the internet and ingesting it.
Latest Cycle - Started Nov. 2020
This cycle has been different for a number of reasons: 1) It came faster than expected. Typically the pain free time between my cycles increases by 3 months each cycle. It is crazy how accurately it has followed it my entire life! 3 months between my first cycle and my second, then six months, then 9 months, etc. etc. I was all the way up to three years between cycles, and then this latest one came after 2.5 years. This was very disappointing for me. 2) It has lasted much longer than

Last night, completed Bust 4 with a 1/2 tab of Vitamin L.  Have not had a legit attack since before Bust 3 but still experience soreness and occasional shadows, so I thought one more Bust would be appropriate.
On the Bust itself:
- significant body discomfort, experienced mostly as jaw and neck tension combined with agitation.  This was not a serious problem, but it was not especially pleasant either.  I paced quite a bit
- lots of visual changes in the form of shifting patterns and breathing floors, walls, etc
All in all,
though I have certainly had some good times, I am
ready for a break from The Vitamin L recreational experience.
medicinal effects:
- during the Bust, experienced occasional pressure in CH area.  This was not significant enough to qualify as a shadow.
That's about it!
The night before last, I went out to a nice dinner with my SO and another couple we've had planned for months.  We had a wine pairing with our dinner, so I had seven different wines, mostly reds, and a sweet wine with dessert.  I mention it because red wine is an almost guaranteed trigger for me as is anything overly sweet.  The meal passed without even a shadow!  I even had a couple martinis at cocktail hour before dinner.  I had an injection of Imitrex back in my room in case things broke bad, but did not have to think about it.
normally, I wouldn't dream of drinking so much as a beer during or around a CH cycle but with the success of the Vitamin L and D, I was able to enjoy myself.  It's a different world.  We've spent the past month wondering if we should cancel this dinner because of my cycle; quite empowering to go through with it like this.
*****
if anybody is reading this looking for advice:
-start the MM or L as soon as you can, and space them apart 5 days, per protocol.  I did 4 to avoid a Christmas Day Bust but I think 5 would have been better
-start the Vitamin D regime immediately. I eventually doubled my D to 20,000 ius a day but YMMV.  I'm a big guy.
-try aborting attacks with ice cold water and 5 hour energy drinks (these are far superior to any others, because you can bolt them down immediately).
-very strong ginger for shadows and neck massages at cluster spots, specifically, tendons on side of neck and under ear.  Strong coffee helped my shadows as well
-only take Imitrex if it's an emergency.  I have found I get significant increase in cluster attacks following Imitrex use and a marked increase in pain level
-keep a diary and be methodical.   When did your cycle start?  When does each attack start? How long does an attack last?  What did you do to stop it?  Use this to identify possible triggers and possible actions that help you.   It's also something to focus on and be constructive with when you're up in the middle of the night in excruciating pain
-find a doctor who will prescribe oxygen for you. It is not expensive out of pocket or through insurance.  Advocate for yourself on this or ask someone to help you advocate.

To my wonderful Cluster Buster Community,
I want to share my success story with busting thanks to the help and advice of many fellow cluster heads, specifically
@xxx
,
@CHfather
,
@Bejeeber
, and
@Shaun brearley
, who took the time to answer my many questions and to guide me in my journey.  Thanks to all of you, I asked my Functional Medicine doctor what she thought of the busting idea (she is an alternative doc who left Mayo to go out on her own), and she referred me to a patient of hers who could provide me with mushrooms. It involved crossing state lines into Michigan from Wisconsin and a 7.5-hour drive each way, but it was well worth it. I only got halfway before I had to stop and get a hotel so I could use my oxygen and get off the road for the 6 attacks I knew I would have before morning. I was able to make it back the next afternoon before the attacks began again. I waited another week to get all of the meds out of my system to do my first bust. I did .4 grams the first time. I could not believe how great I felt. I would not say I was hallucinating but what I didn't expect was that the mushrooms would take away my shadows and general low-grade pain I had between attacks. I felt amazing. I had forgotten what it felt like to not be in pain. I was so relieved I was in tears.
The first night I had 6 attacks, but each night after that, they became less severe and fewer. After the one-week waiting period, I did another bust with .6 grams. Again I felt great. From there, I gradually improved, and within a few days, my shadows were gone, and I was done with my cycle! It felt like a miracle, and I never even had to do a third bust. That was almost 3 months ago, and I am still cluster-free.
I was also able to get extra mushrooms which are dried and frozen, so I know that if a cycle returns, I have what I need to heal myself. That knowledge has made me much less anxious and makes this condition less of a tragedy for me.
I would never have had the knowledge or confidence to do a bust had it not been for this trusted group of amazing people! Thank you from the bottom of my heart. This group is saving lives and is literally the only resource that is truly knowledgable and willing to share their personal experiences and information.
I truly hope that there is legal access to hallucinogenics soon and that the medical community accepts this as a legitimate treatment.
You are my heroes!

Hi Everyone,
I just wanted to share my story and my Miracle. I was diagnosed with CH 3 years ago and was subsequently labeled Refractory.  I was cycling into a Chronic state when I found Ketamine infusions. For 3 years I had experienced severe attacks 6-8x day for 2-3 hours each attack. Every treatment I tried and had any success with eventually stopped working. I was severely depressed with daily Anxiety and PTSD symptoms and had not been able to work or honestly function at all for 2 years. I went into the Ketamine clinic with absolutely no hope or belief that it would help me at all. It is hard to put into words all that I have experienced since my first infusion, but I can say this, it is an absolute Miracle for me and has completely stopped the CH attacks. Not only have I been in complete Remission, but also, I have had all symptoms of depression  and anxiety eliminated. The Ketamine clinic I use has been open since 2008 and my doctor reports better results using Ketamine on CH patients than Migraine or Treatment Resistant Headache patients. I was actively having a CH attack during my second Ketamine infusion and it not only aborted the attack but it also put me into immediate remission and I remain in remission today. I had a total of 7 higher dose Ketamine infusions lasting 2.5 hours each over a 2 week period. I now take Naltrexone 4.5 mg daily and Ketamine Lozenges 75mg daily and have a Ketamine booster IV when needed (which is about every 4-6 weeks at this time). I am going on 4 months and I can only say that I feel completely different than I did during the short remissions I experienced in the last 3 years. I am slowly coming back to life. I know that only those who suffer or have suffered with this unbelievably horrific disorder can understand. I know that what I have experienced is a true miracle for me and I know beyond a shadow of a doubt that I am alive today because of Ketamine. I hope this reaches someone who needs to hear this. I believe this could be a game changer for many CH patients. I am Grateful to the core of my being for every day I have had free from the Monster. I am a Survivor...I am a Miracle.

First post but have been reading the board for years. I wanted to share what has been working for me for the past year and a half. Usual caveats- not a doctor, your mileage may vary, and this is just my experience.
For some background, I have had cluster headaches for going on 12 years. Every year has a minimum of two episodes that last 8-10 weeks. Each episode will have typically 2-3 headaches per day. Emgality and verapamil didn’t prevent or stop clusters for me. I have had strong side effects from medicines like sumatriptan and Topamax and refused to use them any longer.
I tried the clusterbuster program and had mild success on my on days, but immediately fell back into headaches on the days off with no lasting relief.
Last year I tried the below supplement routine and have used it for my last three cycles. I have had shadows on the program but have been headache free for 18 months so far. It’s based on research showing low allopregnanolone levels in cluster headache patients and theorizing that the low levels are a part of the headache process
Treatment Plan:
I start at first sign of headaches or shadows.
Reducing the Severity of Headaches and Shadows:
aka preventing the trigeminal nerve from getting angry
Cetirizine:
10mg twice daily (h1 receptor antagonist. Low potential to cross blood brain barrier however some research shows it will slightly. Chosen over benedryl due to lack of sedation and cognitive impairment)
Famotidine:
20mg twice daily (h2 receptor antagonist, crosses blood brain barrier)
Quercetin:
500mg twice daily. (reduced global histamine response)
Raising Allopregnanolone levels and improving HTPA function:
to help fill in the gaps where the body is currently failing in production
Vitamin D3 with K2:
10,000mg of Vitamin D3 with 300mg of K2, taken once daily.
Pregnenolone:
50mg once daily to convert to allopregnanolone. Research shows pregnenolone readily converts to allopregnanolone. This can be titrated up as needed to reduce headache frequency and severity. I have gone up to 300mg during a cycle, and there is some research showing that it is well tolerated up to 500mg per day.
DHEA:
50mg daily to encourage the conversion of pregnenolone to allopregnanolone so it doesn’t convert down the testosterone pathway.
Ashwagandha:
250mg sensoril for reduced cortisol
Abortive:
At the first sign of a headache
Caffeine:
from coffee or tea. I avoid caffeine sources such as energy drinks or supplements containing niacin or other vasodilators, which is counter productive since we want the vasoconstriction of caffeine
Every 5 days step down pregnenolone dosage. If shadows return, step back up and repeat.
With this I have been able to keep my headaches away and combat shadows with a cup of coffee.
This is my third cycle using this program and I waited to post it until I had repeat success. I understand that this could be unique to me, however the repeated success I’ve had has made me want to share this protocol in case it can help anyone else.

Couldn't be more relieved to be writing this post, but I'm delighted to report that I'm 9 days pain free, so I am feeling quite confident that the beast is back in the box for the foreseeable. I wasn't able to find a huge number of detailed bust reports for LSD, so I wanted to write up a report that was quite thorough, in case it's useful to someone (even if it's just future me!)
Context: I'm 27, male, based in London. This is my third cycle so far, so I'm still very new to ECH. My first two cycles were two years apart. In my first cycle, I received a diagnosis after 6 weeks, found Custerbusters after 8 weeks, and spent the following 2 weeks on a slightly manic LSD binge, after being quite desperate for some relief. The cycle ended after that, unsurprisingly, though I was never sure if it was the L or the cycle ending naturally.
After two years pain free, it returned in 2017. In that cycle, I only had access to MM. It took 5 weeks to break it, but got there in the end. I then had 2 and a half years pain free before my current cycle, which followed a missed maintenance dose and an errant glass of wine in June.
Day 1: My cycle with a relatively manageable K5. I went for a relatively modest ~40ug LSD, mostly sub-hallucinogenic, in accordance with the regimen as it stood in 2015. I experienced mild slapbacks (a pressure building in my forehead) as the effects wore off. In the days that followed, my attacks remained consistent in terms of pain, with frequency staying at/below 1 per day.
Day 5: I got hit with a K8, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. I dosed again afterwards, around ~50ug. When I came down, I was hit with a K8 slapback, and then a K9 the following day. Frequency was now up to around 2-3/day, often with one attack significantly worse than others.
Day 9: Dosed again, this time ~100ug, after coming across more up to date advice which suggested 'recreational doses' for busting with L. I decided to bring the bust forward a day, basically because I had to be functional the next day, but also because in my recreational use I'd found that my tolerance level was usually unnoticeable after 3 days off. Perhaps this was a bad call though, as I got hit with 2 and a half hours of K10, hands down the worst attack of my life. Decided after this that I needed to get hold of some O2, as the caffeine and hot showers weren't particularly helpful anymore.
Day 12: Finally was able to start the D3 regimen. Also started cutting chocolate from my diet and stopped skipping meals, as I began to suspect these were triggers.
Day 16: After several days of battling my doctor for it, I finally got my hands on some O2. Can't believe I went without it for the past 16 days, and the two cycles before this one! I felt giddy with relief after killing my first attack with it, made a mental note to ensure I had it from day 1 of my next cycle.
Day 17: Dosed again with around~100ug LSD, very slapback heavy trip but super manageable with my tank by my side.

Hey Clusterbusters
My name is Alec and I'm 33 years old. I had my first cluster headache at 12 and was diagnosed at 16 by a neurologist where I live in Australia. I have been a member of several forums a few years back but haven't really been active in support groups for the last 5 years or so and I need to vent. I saw the "Beast" documentary on YouTube a few days ago and Cluster Busters seems like an awesome group - I'd love to become an active member here and get involved as much as I can. I have just had a KIP 10 cluster attack with a complete absence of pain (see my story below), I could just feel the immense pressure and I can tell what pain level it is even though it's masked. I haven't had a KIP10 in around 6 months or so and it literally just went away, so I'm still pumping full of adrenalin and just want to talk a bit about my last few months.
When I was diagnosed at 16, I attempted the full array of then first and second line treatment options. All the standard medications such as verapamil, triptans etc. My headaches never responded to any preventatives or abortives and as a last resort I was placed on opioids. At first I was taking codeine and then graduated to oxycodone. I want to make it clear from the outset that I do not recommend opioids for cluster headaches sufferers, but every situation is unique and for me opioids became my primary way of coping with my headaches. Of course, the dose continually increased and to be completely honest up until around a month ago they never really provided me with much relief from the physical pain at all. They did help me markedly with the emotional distress and turmoil associated with treatment-refractory cluster headaches which unfortunately for me became chronic at around 25 years old.
I understand that everybody's perception of pain is different and that some of us have episodic clusters and others (like me) have chronic. I have to say though that after doing research on cluster headaches online, either there are many people out there who claim to have the condition who clearly do not OR I happen to suffer a lot more than others. For me, cluster headaches are the worst imaginable pain and trying to describe them is pointless. I do not understand how somebody could have cluster headaches and mistakenly think they have migraines for years before a correct diagnosis - for me, the pain of a cluster headache is so distinct and unique.
The toll these headaches have had on my life is catastrophic. I have been in 2x long-term relationships with people I loved and both ended due to my inability to be a normal person due to my pain. I have tried so hard to maintain employment and have worked in several industries including law enforcement, finance and legal services. All of my attempts at employment have ended due to my pain and I have been on long-term disability now for several years. 21 year Episodic. Clusters used to happen every 9 months, lasted 1 month with 1 or two attack / day lasting 1 hour each. The cycle would be like clockwork, almost to the day. Back then I was treating with maxalt wafers only, and running cold water on my head. Eventually was prescribed amitriptyline, which unsurprisingly didn't do anything. Sometimes my cycle would
end
with a day-long attack. Different from a regular cluster headache, but, still on the cluster side. Really weird. Anyway, I would often end up in the ER with these ending attacks, but, they were always bad news / good news. Bad because I knew they would last at least 24 hours; and were brutal (not as bad as a CH attack, but, pretty unbearable due to the length of the attack and no sleep). Good because I knew the cycle would end after that one weird one. Eventually saw my first neurologist in 2012 after a trip to the ER (10 years after my CHs started). I've had all of the required brain scans, etc. and got the official diagnosis. Neurologist recommended maxalt wafers, home oxygen, prednisone taper at the start of a cycle, and verapamil for the duration of the cycle and for 6 weeks after it clears. Family doctor wouldn't prescribe me any of those things except for Maxalt. Said everything else was too risky.
Found a new Family Doctor 2 years later (2014). Got me hooked up with another Neurologist, and got the same treatment plan recommended. Got all of my scripts filled, etc.
Over the years, my cycles spread out longer (longer remission), but, more attacks while in cycle. One cycle, I didn't realize the risks of too many triptans (was taking 2, 3, sometimes 4 per day that cycle). Ended up getting really bad rebound headaches. Cycle lasted much longer, and I ended up getting upwards of 8 attacks per day. After that cycle, and realizing what had happened; I've tried to avoid the triptans whenever possible. I rely on home o2, and only use a maxalt nasal spray if I'm out and don't have access to o2 or any other options.
Started the D3 regimen in 2019, and have been taking all of the cofactors and everything daily since then.
For the last few cycles, my treatment plan has been:
1) 13 day prednisone taper at the onset of a cycle; 60mg 2 days, 50mg 3 days, 40mg 3 days, 30mg 2 days, 20mg 2 days, 10mg 1 day
2) Verapamil - 120mg x 3 / day
3) Home o2 - 25lpm through Cluster o2 mask (coupled with the odd sip of really cold ice water through a straw)
4) Rizatriptan Nasal spray only for emergencies (5mg)
5) Still doing the D3 regimen
6) Have tried adding melatonin in before bed (10mg)
7) Energy drinks to try to help clear shadows
8) Ice-pack on the head for shadows, and sometimes during attacks (other times I can't stand it)
9) No alcohol
The prednisone taper has killed 2 cycles right at the start. One cycle it didn't completely kill it (the cycle above with the triptan overuse); and I ended up doing a 2nd prednisone taper, which did end it

I will be 62 this year. I have lived with cluster headaches since 1970. I remember my first episode occurred in 10th grad Spanish class. I had zero idea what was happening all I know is I had to get out of class fast and hide..somewhere. I had no idea this was to be the start of a lifelong journey through the the worst pain I have ever endured. As the episodes would start it became harder to hide them and of course my family had me tested for all the underlying cause and brain tumor was ruled out. So since no doctor could conclude what was causing my headache I pretty much gave up and decided that as long as they would eventual disappear I would just deal with it.
Over the years I got pretty good at figuring out the timing or the onset . Yes they were definitely cyclical, seasonal. In my 's 30's and 40's I could count on once a year for a period of 6-7 weeks I would have to deal with the pain.
I also became pretty good at hiding the pain. It's unfortunately a pretty misunderstood disorder and because of that there is a certain amount of shame involved, after all it's just a headache , we all get them.
The fear is that they will occur when you can't disappear and work though the pain. Like the first date with my wife of 38 years. At a drive in to see Jaws. It's a wonder she ,married me. God you feel like a jerk writhing in pain eye watering and then exhaustion when it finally eases up.
Anyhow writing this is my way of finally deciding not to hide and ride them out. Fortunately I am in a position with my work that as the boss I can disappear if needed and not too many people will question it. So when the need arises I will find a car in the lot out of the way and settle in for an hour of hurt then collect myself and resume my day.
I the thing I can't control is being in public places or events or parties or dinner with friends when I have no escape route. No place to hide. So for the cluster period I choose  not to put myself in that position and my wife lives the same life until they pass.
I just wrote cluster period,and for 40 some years I can tell you I avoided even using the word cluster in a sentence. The mere mention scares me and not much does. I have read all kinds of description about the pain of cluster headaches but for me it's relentless. It takes you to point of no more and continues to give you more. Rocking moaning all part of the process. I think the stigma is the worst and this why I have chosen to hide this condition and never acknowledge to anyone other than my wife what this has done to life. Don't get me wrong when not in cycle I'm the last guy to even think about it and oddly enough I never get headaches outside of these monsters.
While writing this it strikes me that although I have had C H for 40 years or better the pain memory fades other than a few bastard ones that leave a lasting scar.

Hi there!
I am new to the board and tbh kind of new to clusters as well.
So let me introduce myself.
My name is Pieter-Jan, Belgian, 33YO, former mechanic, whisky-lover
and this nickname used to be quite a well-known name in BMW-forums of Europe.
Never thought I'd use the name in this context, but here I am.
I'm probably quite lucky compared to a lot of you guys.
It all started in Octobre 2014, with a 'severe' headache. I never had any headaches,
and yes, I am one of those guy who thought migraine is a lame excuse to stay home from work. Boy, was I wrong.
But then, I had a few of those attacks and they were bad enough to make me go to a doctor.
I had an MRI with nothing to be found. the attacks stopped after a few and i stopped worrying about it.
Some time later a belgian CH-patient is shown in the media and i got to know the term 'clusterheadache',
which me me wonder if that could have been what i experienced.
I shrugged it off and told myself not to be a baby, it was impossible my attacks were the same as such a serious condition.
The more time passed, the more I knew i was being a crybaby back in the day.
Until March 24 of this year, an easy sundayeve with the family.
At 8pm it struck. Excruciating pain, stabbing in the back of my eye, me trying to push my eye in my head,
till I was crying, rolling around in the coach.
At 9.30pm it stopped, completely gone. my eye looked like a blood vessel had popped, my skin red and hot,
but that was all the evidence left.
That moment I knew it was the same thing like 2014, googled it and found a sh*load of information about it.
The descriptions were a perfect fit.
exactly one week later i had the proof, cause there was the second attack.
A few days later they started daily and before the next weekend multiple times/day and at night.
I'll spare you guys the doctor stories, you all know how those go.
I was lucky to be taken serious and got to a neurologist within 3 days.
He listened to my story and took a seat.
If you know, you know In Europe doctors are trained to bring 'bad news' such as patients passing, cancer, serious deseases... etc.
Step 1 of this 'bad news conversation' is to sit down next to the patient.
After some explanation he got right to it and gave me a GON-injection.
which started working after 2 days.
I didn't have (severe) pain from the attacks. I still feel they are happening and feel an occasional stab in the eye,
but I can handle the pain.
One thing that's harder to handle is the shadow. Since the injection I have an almost constant headache at the level of a brainfreeze.
Verapamil helped a little bit but made my legs swell very fast to the point my knee hurts enough to make me limp.
Caffeine-gum and energydrinks don't do anything, except from keeping me awake at night.
Since I read a lot of stories I decided maybe i don't have to suffer for years like a lot of you...
Maybe i don't have to try 50 different medicines..
I already ordered some truffles. Maybe it's not enough since i ordered

I have battled CH on and off for 13 years
it was only this past year that I realized cluster headaches are in fact a real thing and are in fact what's wrong with me. I have been trying to help myself for so long. treating with every kind of over the counter medication you can think of. always with no success. no real reliable relief. finally this year during my spring cycle things got so bad I wound up in the ER. more than once.  I never would have imagined clusters could do that to me.
that's what it took for me to realize what this was. all my life I thought I was sort of over reacting or being weak about something normal people go through with little trouble. as a kid in school I just had to put my head down and bare it. occasionally I would get some time in the clinic or sent home early. growing up it has always caused trouble with work. I don't like to look like a complainer or weak or someone who makes excuses. so I have always kept my mouth shut and soldiered through what I had to. and when I couldn't bare it, quietly submit to the pain at home before passing out on over the counter pain meds. anything to stop the pain.
I have finally come to terms with living with ch. with that I hope I can be of some help to others who may need it
There are 3 key things I have found to help me through an attack or a cycle
STAY CALM!
I have found that panic can make the pain more severe and during a more severe attack, especially if a remedy fails you, you are more likely to panic. this can quickly become a vicious cycle. stay calm soldier on.
OXYGEN!
high flow oxygen usually counters an attack in about 20 minutes if I catch it at the right time. since Ive accepted my diagnosis from a neurologist I was lucky enough to get oxygen prescribed to me from a doctor at a free clinic. I did this without any insurance and was able to find a local oxygen vendor who also deals with the general public.
MAGIC MUSHROOMS
these are last on the list for a reason. that reason mainly being their legal status. make no mistake these things will change your life. obviously not for kids and use them at your own discretion. that being said I have found incredible relief dosing with MM. sometimes short lived but still better than anything over the counter.
in the past before I knew what I was going through I would try anything and everything to combat an attack. I had a few little rituals and tricks but the main goal was usually to get home or  to a dark room so I could try to relax an eventually go to sleep.
If none of those 3 key options work for you these are a few things I have used in the past to help find relief
caffeine
- this was always a go to when I needed to buy some time. chugging an energy drink soda or coffee always saved me for a few hours
bengay
- in recent years I sometimes use bengay or similar topical ointment. rubbing it on liberally over my shoulders neck and even my face, around my temples and sinus'. this helped to relax and relieve some nasal pressure
spic

A number of years ago, I unfortunately had recurring episodes of some awful cluster headaches. I’d previously described them on a now-deleted blog, and I’m posting here in case this is in any way useful to other sufferers going forward.
I’d first like to stress that:
I am not a doctor or healthcare professional.
Every human body is different.
We each react to supplements in very different ways.
What works for one sufferer may not work for another.
I have no affiliation, previous employment, or investment in Holland & Barrett.
As of December 2025, I’m a 45 year-old white male in good physical/mental health.
My first experience of cluster headaches was at the age of 20 (year 2000), at a time when we were starting to take advantage of what the WWW could offer us, but without the massive amount of data, research and shared experiences that we have in 2025.
Those first episodes hit me hard in the summer of 2020, and my GP at the time told me that they must be migraines, and that I should just take ibuprofen. I got a second opinion, and when the doctor in question said that the episodes sounded more like cluster headaches it felt like I was finally being heard and understood.
The regularity of the cluster headaches was both a curse and a blessing. They’d typically last between 10 and 20 minutes each whilst I was at work, and at almost the same time every day. The intensity of them, and the way in which I went from feeling nothing to agony was frightening. The regularity did at least mean that I could prepare. I tried espresso shots, throwing back a can of Red Bull, and/or running up multiple flights of stairs to varying degrees of success. After a few months of suffering, they’d go again until the next year.
From memory, I believe I had a couple of years without any episodes between 2005 and 2007. They came back hard in the summer of 2007 lasting 15 to 20 minutes at a time, and sometimes twice a day. In desperation I emailed Peter Goadsby (Professor of Neurology at King's College London) for advice. He said that he’d be willing to see me if my GP referred me. Around the same time I’d read on a CH forum that some sufferers had found different levels of success in taking supplements of taurine (found in Red Bull), calcium, magnesium and kudzu. In desperation I started taking the lot. I did ask Prof. Goadsby in that initial email if there were safe dosage limits that I should adhere to, but he didn’t acknowledge the question and I rightly/wrongly took that as a green light to carry on. 500mg - taurine
Combined 500mg calcium/250mg magnesium
500mg of kudzu
I took these together daily.

st

Hi friends. This is going to be quite the wall of text, for a few reasons.
Partly to thoroughly explain my situation. Secondly there may be things in here that can help others.
But mainly, just to get things off my chest, or more aptly, out of my head.
I'll probably do this in 2 parts to break it up. First, I'll describe my background.
44 male from Australia. Suffered from episodic cluster headaches for about 15 years.
As with almost all of us sufferers, I have researched the hell out of this over the years, including here.
My cycle is a pretty standard one : I will get 8 weeks of daily clusters, and then a remission period of 1.5 years.
The first 2-3 cycles, i was pretty unaware. I was younger then, and simply battled through.
My go to was ( and always has ) a boiling hot shower, directly on the head, and rocking back and forward.
They hit like clockwork, once per day at 7-8pm every night, lasting around an hour.
I have a very high pain tolerance, but when these things struck, it was an eye opener.
I wasnt even aware a pain like this could exist. Things i used to think were a 7/10 on the pain scale, suddenly became a 3/10.
Its actually made it hard now, whenever somebody asks what a pain level is for a separate injury, im going off my cluster scale, because i know what a real 10/10 pain is now. My 4/10 pain, is their 8/10 pain. Anyway...
On about the 3rd cycle, it struck while I was out camping. Alone. In the middle of nowhere in the bush.
It was horrific. It was at this time, in desperation, I resorted to something i created out of desperation, called the rocking technique.
What i did, was kneel in the dirt (ground), lean forward and put my head on the ground in my hands.....and just rocked backwards and forwards.....and I counted each rock. As i said, I was desperate, with no other options...
It was during this, that I discovered, although it was very painful, if I kept counting, I never got to 500, before i felt it start to ebb away.
At 1 rock per second roughly, this was around 10 minutes or so.
I'd just completed my first abort! During the next attack, i was able to replicate it again.
Although still suffering up to a 7/10, it felt like a win.
But....
As I have discovered over the years, when you defeat the beast, it seems to piss it off. It tries to claw its way around your methods, or find a new way to punish you. I was about to find this out for the first time.
In the following days, my clusters stopped being like clock work. They started attacking at random times.
Worse still, my one attack a day, had now become 2-3 per day. Since that day, it has never returned to normal during a cycle.
Sometimes i wish i hadnt pissed ithe beast off, and just had the standard 1 per night, at a time I knew.
Regardless, for the remainder of that cycle, i was able to abort about 75% of the attacks using the rocking technique.
A year and a half passed, and the beast was back. Worse still, my rocking technique was far less effective.

I'm a 50 y/o Dutch guy and each spring and fall I suffer from CH.
My preferred method of aborting an attack is using psilocybine which is the working compound in so called magic mushrooms and truffles.
See:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Psilocybin
I have been visiting this forum for some time now and I noticed that a lot of people seek practical information about using or obtaining shrooms or truffles from Holland.
With this post I will try and give those who seek information about the subject some quick answers and hopefully you'll find them usefull.
All the information below is factual and/or based on personel experience.
You can read it as an rough guide.
If you need more information or help in any way just ask or send me a PM.
Is it legal to buy Magic Mushrooms in Holland?
No.
Since 2008 it is prohibited to buy or sell Magic Mushrooms.
Is it legal to buy mushroom spores or growboxes in Holland?
Yes.
To grow your own mushrooms you can either buy spores or just simple buy a complete growbox.
A growbox is a complete set with instructions and really easy to do.
Spores and/or growbox are legal to be sold and possessed by people of 18 years and older in the Netherlands by Dutch law.
Is it legal to buy truffles in Holland?
Yes.
Since the goverment introduced a ban on mushrooms they forgot to include truffles.
Truffles are legal to be sold and possessed by people of 18 years and older in the Netherlands by Dutch law.
Where can I buy truffles, spores or growboxes?
There are a lot of so called smartshops where you can buyÂ  truffles, spores or growboxes.
You will find a smartshop in almost every major Dutch city or on-line.
What is the best smartshop to visit?
All smartshops buy their spores, growboxes or truffles from the same growers/wholesalers.
It all comes down to freshness of the product. The shop with the biggest turn-over usually sells the best quality.
Can I mail order truffles, spores or growboxes?
Most web-based smartshops will send you your order but with a disclaimer stating that you are responsible for informing yourself on the legal status, import and custom laws of these products in your country.
Is there a difference between truffles and mushrooms?
For aborting or trying to bust CH there is no difference.
Both truffles and mushrooms contain psiclocybine in a more or less same amount and the effects are similar.
Is there a difference between the various truffel products offered?
Smartshops sell truffles with all kinds of fancy names and with al kinds of alleged specific properties. Mostly this is just marketing.
For mushrooms there are different species and some are believed to be more potent then others.
From a botanical point of view this is true but remember truffles and mushrooms are a product of nature. The conditions of growing, the exact time of picking and freshness have an huge impact on the individual potency.
For aborting or trying to bust CH ANY magic mushroom or truffle will do.

I've been wanting to post on this for a while now...This is probably going to be a long complicated post, most likely MORE than one post...but here goes
Harmala Alkaloids are substances found in varying degrees in plants like Syrian Rue, Banisteriopsis caapi vine, Passionflower and Tobacco (I'm sure there are more)Â  The two with the highest known amounts are the Syrian Rue seeds and the Banisteriopsis Caapi vines.
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmala_alkaloid
The major alkaloids involved are Harmine, Harmaline, and Tetrahydroharmine.Â  These are all Beta Carbolines, They all inhibit the MAO A enzyme, and they all are considered RIMA's--Reversible Inhibitors of MAO subtype A.Â (For more info on RIMA's you can look into the pharmaceutical anti-depressant moclobomide, which has MAO A enzyme as it's mechanism of action.)Â  Â All 3 substances are completely legal in the U.S., as are both the Caapi Vine and Rue seeds.Â  I know I have read that they are hitting some of the same receptors as Psilocybin and LSA, but right now I can't find my sources.
But first we'll touch on MAO inhibitors in general.Â  Everything you hear about them sounds scary, everything seems to say, "Do Not Mix With MAO inhibitors"Â  We need to know that the substances contained in Caapi vines and Syrian Rue seeds are very different drugs than the old style pharmaceutical MAO inhibitors that were usually used for depression.Â  These older MAO inhibitors would inhibit TWO enzymes, MAO A and MAO B, where as the RIMA's only inhibit ONE enzyme, MAO A.Â  This is important because that means you still have an active MAO B enzyme, which makes your body perfectly able to still metabolize Tyramine.Â  With the older MAO inhibitors that inhibited BOTH enzymes you are not able to metabolize tyramine, this can lead to hypertensive crisis, and this is the biggest warning you will hear about with MAO inhibiting drugs.Â  Just remember--THIS IS NOT AN ISSUE WITH THE NATURAL MAO A INHIBITING DRUGS LIKE RUE OR CAAPI VINES.Â  The biggest thing that made me more comfortable with these drugs was when I realized that I have been taking them all my life without knowing it.Â  Certain foods, like chocolate, have this effect naturally andÂ  you'll never notice it.Â  Â Â In fact, Terence Mckenna claims that ALL tryptamines are RIMA's--this means the mushrooms, LSD, and LSA containing seeds that we are taking are all having this effect on us.Â  I can confirm this effect from the experience of smoking DMT while on mushrooms.Â  (More on that later)
So no risk of "hypertensive crisis"Â  Just forget about it.Â  There is the theory that if you are on RIMA's and eat lots and lots of tyramine containing foods that eventually you could "overwhelm" the system and cause this crisis, but I have NEVER heard of this actually happening.Â  From what I've read it seems more like people have been scared of MAO inhibitors for a long time and are reluctant to let go of that fear.Â  One of the biggest sources of tyramine is chees

I am new here and wanted to share my experience with CH.  This site has truly changed my life and I want to return the favor for anyone that comes along.
I am 40 and have had migraines all of my life.  It runs in my family.  Large doses of caffeine and/or sumatriptan would get rid of these quickly and it didn't happen often.  No biggie - 5 time a year on average.
However, 5 years ago, I started experiencing a type of pain I didn't know even existed.  I have been to the E.R., had many MRI's, visits with headache specialists, neurologists, chiropractors, and nut jobs.  At no point did anyone know what was going on with me.  I was told it was just a migraine.  No sir!! I know what a migraine is.  Those are bad but this...this is worse than anything I can imagine.  This type of pain made me cry in front of my kids...sigh..wish that hadn't happened!  Made me want to physically hurt anyone who spoke to me.  It wasn't until this year that I found this site and wow!  Things have changed.
Year 1
- Started late summer.
- Pain levels of 8-10 ever night at 7pm almost to the dot.
- Could sleep.
- Terrible "shadow" for the entire day.  Repeat cycle.
- Went to doctor after doctor.  Sumatriptan helped reduce most pain 2 or 3 points down.
- During one attack I noticed how stuffy my nose was and that I couldn't breathe.  I was desperate.  Found an old bottle of Afrin and almost emptied the bottle in my nose.  Attack gone in 15 minutes.
- 7 months into it I went to an ENT.  Said I needed surgery.  I did.  Doctor aggressively trimmed down my turbinates. 2 weeks later - all attacks gone.
Year 2
- nada.  Nothing.  Life was awesome.
Year 3
- Year 1 repeated itself but on the other side.  Went back to ENT, had another surgery.  Same results.
Year 4
- nada.  Nothing.  Life was again normal.  Had some attacks that seemed random but mostly triggered by alcohol.  It took me a while to realize that was a trigger.  Saw neuro who said I had CH but he wasn't sure that was even a real thing.  Gave me Sumatriptan.  Whatever.  Attacks were on and off throughout the year and it was bearable.
Year 5 (current)
- started having small attacks after drinking with friends.  I immediately knew what was going down.
- by Mid-August I was having 5 attacks/day. (post wakeup, 3ish, 7ish, 2am waking from sleep.  Pain levels of 6,7,10,8 respectively.  Each one lasting 2-3 hours.  I am not sure life could be worse at the moment.
- by end of August I was taking 5 times the recommended dose of Sumatriptan.  It was enough to keep me sane but I used 30 days worth in 1 week.  Doc refused to refill.
- Desperate, I started searching and found this site.  I can't tell you how many times I read through these forums while pacing through my house.  Called my PCP and demanded an appointment immediately.  They told me it would be 4-5 weeks.  I called again after 2 days and begged.  They saw me the next day.  I asked for o2.  Doc was confused but wrote a script anyway.
Today, I am almost completely pain free f

Dear Clusterheads,
The time has come to introduce myself to our beloved clusterheads family.
I was observing You for long time, but now I feel obliged to give my input.
My name is Radoslaw and I'm from Poland, suffering for CH since 2016 (maybe 2015, I don't remember properly). First two years without proper diagnose, but with small 1-2 weeks episodes during autumn each year. In 2018 I had 4 weeks terrible headaches, so I started my journey through neurologists. With help form the web knowledge I made a diagnose which was later confirmed by doctors - cluster headaches. Firstly doctors proposed me verapamil, but after cardiac research they refused to give it to me because of bradicardia ( a lot of wrestling and boxing in the teenage years made my hearth rate about 50-55). I didn't want to take lithium and gabapentin because of side effects ( no other therapy was known here in PL)
In 2019 in June my cluster appeared once again, stronger than before, so I desperately tried to find new solution. I found new doctor ( who isn't neurologist but is a specialist in headaches - brilliant guy !). Together we decided to take a risk and start with verap 480, under direct control of electro-cardio-diagrams. BOOM ! worked perfectly. My hearth rate felt down to 45-50 ( during night even to 40) but I felt good, without side effects, so I decided to continue it. For next 2 years I even forgot that I have this terrible disease ( for my bad...). Unfortunately I didn't stop taking verap after cluster period, but stayed on it for the whole year. In 2021 each attempt to reduce verap caused some shadows and single attacks, so my doctor suspected that I went to chronic version ( today I'm wondering that maybe I'm not chronic, but such long period of verap caused attacks when I tried to stop it - slowly as recommended).
In 2022 in June ( when I was on 480 verap) cluster hit with double power. I increased verap to 960, but it was very risky. It took 4 weeks to stop the cluster but I got also atrial block 1 stage at the end of cycle. I felt really dizzy, so 960 is little too much for me. Since that time, when I was on 480 some single attacks appeared during the year.
Today I'm 34 and I'm really concerned about using verapamil with bradicardia for such a long time. I have concerns wbout hearth pacemaker in the future, so for that reason I decided month ago to stop with verap and to go with regime D and busting with witamin L.
I know the oxygen method, I have 3 tanks at home with proper flowmeter and 1 small tank that I have always in my car. This is my first choise for attack. I'm using also a lot of caffeine in energy drinks.

First CH's started November of 2015.  Thought they were a result of a hangover, caffeine withdrawals, or dehydration.  True cycles started to emerge in spring 2016.  Misdiagnosed by ER as Migraine.  Correctly diagnosed as CH later.  Went through the cycle of preventative and abortives.  Benefits were often limited, side effects were intolerable.  Methodically tried elimination diets, med shifts, etc.  Most with some degree of correlation, but none with lasting relief.  Latest preventative was visiting a local chiropractor.  He noted in Chiropractic school, a member of his cohort was a CH suffer and they focused a bit of Chiropractic intervention with success. I gave it a try.  (Will add notes at the bottom on the impact of other meds)
When engaging chiropractor, I chose to not disclose much beyond CH.  He ran an x-ray panel and review with me.  In his review, he commented that the misalignment of C1 to C3, and other issues in the cervical vertebrae and other markers would lead him to believe I had suffered whiplash ~10 years earlier.  I had not disclosed in Dec of 2002, I had a head-on collision that I chose not to pursue medical treatment for.  He proceeded with a 3-month cycle of adjustments, 3x weekly, with a focus on addressing cervical issues.
In the 3 month cycle, which started about 2 weeks after the start of my CH cycle, the headaches stopped almost entirely.  3 months in, I have a few breaks through CHs,  average 1 or fewer per day (which is miraculous as on cycle I am consistently at 3x daily, on a schedule [2:31 am, 6:00 am, and 2:00 pm]).  I've noted that, when I miss the Chiro adjustments (due to work, etc) greater than 4 days, there is a higher potential to suffer more than 1 CH.
As we all know, CH triggers and resolution is mostly about correlation, as causality is not yet well established.  But thus far, chiropractic adjustments, coupled with stretches, shoulder and neck muscle exercises focusing on extension / relaxation, with heat on neck seem to have demonstrated the most effective treatment path for prevention without side effects.
Hope it helps. I am in Beaverton Oregon and see a chiro here.  I can provide a referral if interested.

Back to start

I felt compelled to share my story after seeing Bob Wold present at Horizons 2009 tonight on vimeo. This is my first post. I welcome your comments. Please forgive me if I have inadvertently violated any forum rules or guidelines, or if I am repeating common knowledge.
NB: I am not a doctor. I am an opinionated total stranger writing under a pseudonym. Nothing I am saying could or should be construed as medical advice.
I started getting severe headaches when I was very young. One day, when I was about five, my parents gave me the usual 2 acetaminophen and sent me to bed. About 20 minutes later, I came out of my room in tears. Exasperated, they wanted to know why. I told them because I couldn't handle the pain, and that if felt like someone was stabbing me in the eyes. Off to the doctor we went.
There was never really an answer to the headache question. At first, I was prescribed Fioricet (acetaminophen+butalbital+caffeine).  The headaches were called tension, then migraine, then cluster, then migraine. I was more interested in ending the pain then having an accurate label.
I do also get normal headaches, which are a totally different beast. No eye pain, no light sensitivity. I pop 2 aspirin+acetaminophen+caffeine and go about my day. If they get bad but not really bad, my favorite self-remedy is standing in a warm shower in a dark bathroom with my face directly under the spray until it stops. I also do this during really bad ones.
Some of the worse headaches were the ones that woke me up. I would usually be somewhat confused; and I would often spend a while in bed in terrible pain before I would get up and take pills. Sometimes, the pain would be entangled in a dream, and I would think if I could solve a math problem, or dial a phone, or repot a plant (I can remember those three dreams to this day, and they happened between 10 and 25 years ago), the pain would stop.
Later in life, they switched me to Zomig (zolmitriptan). The Zomig works ~75% of the time. When it doesn't work, it makes the pain worse. Currently, my insurance won't pay for Zomig, and so I have sumatriptan (Imitrex), which I haven't had to try yet, as I have one Zomig left.
When I was about 19, I entered another stage. The headaches became more common and lasted longer. The pain was worse.
I distinctly remember the worse one I ever had (and I bet you do, too.) It was 1999, and I was 21. The pain started when I was at work, about 6 hours from the end of my shift. Stupidly, I had no pills with me. I was already in trouble over attendance, so I wanted to tough it out. I sat at my computer, and the pain got worse and worse. Finally, with 15 minutes left in my shift, I had to leave immediately, or I was going to vomit all over my cubicle. They said they would have to write me up. I said I didn't care.
I drove 115 miles per hour down the highway to get home. I do not normally drive above 75. I knew I might hit and kill someone. I didn't care. I was crying. I was shifting the position of m

Hello folks. I have been lingering around this board and the clusterheadaches.com board since 2001. It was at that time, when I was 16 years old, I was diagnosed with clusterheadache. It is hard to imagine that soon I will have had these things for half of my lifetime, and have still not found the best treatment for me. I guess it is just an evolution. I hope to use this thread to detail my work thus far to combat my current cycle, as well as the work I still have ahead of me. Feel free to comment, questions, follow along, whatever you'd like.
My Background Previous to Current Cycle: (Feel free to skip over)
I had what I thought were migraines only ever once in a while (one every few years) as a young child. Didn't think a whole lot about it. Then I had them three days in a row in 2001 at the exact same time each day and thought "Holy shit". I thought I was going to die. I was fortunate enough to receive a CH diagnosis right then from my family practitioner. He prescribed a prednisone taper, Inderal, and Zomig. The Prednisone and the inderal killed the cycle. I had a few more cycles over the next year and this medication mix worked just fine. Then things got more interesting:
- Spring of 2003 I entered a cycle and the meds were not working as well. I tried a whole bunch of different ones, per the advice of a neurologist. Nothing worked and I felt like a zombie... at the age of 17
Â  Through my own research I found out about busting. I used MM and did not get another attack until January 2004.
- Winter 2004: I used MM and killed the cycle in its tracks.
- Fall of 2004: I entered a cycle that was not responding to MM. I can't remember exactly how the cycle ended. I do remember this being an extremely agonizing period for me. I also started using oxygen at this time, however, I was not using it properly and therefor didn't receive much relief with it.
- Fall of 2005: I had high hopes about Kudzu at this time. For some reason thought this would be my silver bullet. When it was not working, I can't remember what else I was turning to as a preventative. I do remember this cycle being extremely agonizing. I remember doing crazy stuff like running through the freezing cold in a tank top and slamming energy drinks.

I am 47 years old and I have been suffering almost daily, and typically 2 or 3 times a day, from headaches on the right side of my head for six and a half months now.  First I had my teeth checked, then got a new prescription in my glasses.  Neither helped... Most often they initiate at a point just above the temple but slightly closer to my eye and about 1/2 inch beneath my skull.  It is a sharp point source that radiates outward and it is almost always in the exact same location.  I was initially treating the headaches with Ibuprofen and was tricked into believing that it helped, because the headaches would only last 30 or 40 minutes and I was attributing the relief to the Ibuprofen.  I suspected that the Ibuprofen had stopped working so I moved on to Naproxen then started stacking Extra Strength Tylenol.  After about 2 months of constant suffering I ended up resorting to the ER, where I had CT scans both with and without contrast.  Both were negative.  I then found a new doctor and was treated with oral Prednisone 40mg which had no effect on the headache, but turned off my ability to control myself, culminating in smashing my guitar.  So 6 days into the Prednisone I messaged my doctor and he told me to stop the Prednisone.  Subsequently, we tried Sumatriptan Nasal Spray 20mg.  I did not get relief so I gave up on it after 2 attempts.  Later, I returned to the Sumatriptan, using 10 doses in 8 days only to realize that I was being tricked again in the same way as I described with the Ibuprofen.  I was then scheduled for an MRI which was also negative.
I had heard about psilocybin mushrooms as a treatment for cluster headaches and have tried several times.  The mushrooms always completely turn the headaches off for the duration, then significantly reduce the intensity, duration and frequency of the attacks for a few days.  Today my doctor finally prescribed oxygen as an abortive and Topamax as a preventative.  I am afraid to start taking the Topamax, as I was only just able to stop taking antidepressants (primarily Zoloft) 20 months ago, after having taken them for 20 years for severe depression.  I know that the Topamax will interfere with psilocybin as well, so I don't think I'm going to take it.  I have high hopes for oxygen therapy but it was a real hassle trying to explain how to write the prescription.  Then, the medical supply company that he gave the order to wanted to fix me up with an oxygen concentrator.  Fortunately, I refused and was able to go back into the office and request a different provider that could supply a type M tank, regulator and mask.  I am waiting on the insurance to approve the treatment....

Most contributors to this forum will be far more knowledgeable than I regarding both cluster headaches and those substances which have not as yet become mainstream weapons for the medical community in the fight against this uniquely painful affliction.
I told my 25-year-old son Michael's story on Clusterbusters sister site, Clusterheadaches. I repeat it here in case it could be of benefit to any sufferers out there looking for others with similar symptoms and histories who might not luck onto, as I did, the sister site. Bonkers is the screen name used on that site as well.
Michael got hit out of the blue one day while taking a shower. It knocked him to the ground, hurt like hell and scared the shit out of him. He was 12 years old. It wasn't the last one. We thought they were migraines since both my mother and I had those from our teens until we outgrew them at around 30. Our family doctor tried a variety of headache medicines on Michael over the next couple of years without success. He referred us to a Neurologist who, after conducting the usual tests, diagnosed the headaches as Clusters. He tried most, if not all, of the standard cluster remedies. Imitrex seemed to work best, but it too lost effectiveness over time. It got to the point where 2 shots did nothing. Michael got the most relief, and continues to get the most relief, from narcotics. Most of the time they deaden the pain enough that he doesn't black out. He's currently taking Methadone. Unlike the other narcotics, it hasn't required continually increasing dosages to be effective. The neuro continued experimenting with the various cluster remedies, including narcotics, until Michael was about 20 years old.
Then, he got into a particularly nasty car accident. He was life-flighted to the hospital with head injuries. Nothing too serious, but his headaches increased exponentially in both frequency and severity. He was uncontrollable during the spikes. Luckily, the pain usually increased to the point where he would black out. By now he was in the emergency room every couple of days where they've got some bigger guns. Nothing helped. They had to put him in a straight-jacket and eventually, with consent from the neuro, a drug-induced coma. They kept him there for a week. When Michael emerged from the coma he seemed better for awhile. But then they returned. Frustrated, the neuro decided Michael should take all the medicines at once! I don't think we even said goodbye. Degenerative disc disease and 5 fused vertebrae in my neck had me seeing a neck and back pain specialist who I trusted and considered a very good doctor. I brought my son to see him. He's been caring for Michael for the last 4 or 5 years
Michael's case is rare, I believe, in its severity. Not to trivialize the extreme suffering of others, but Michael's spikes often intensify until they result in his blacking out, sometimes as often as 30 times/day. He can't work. He can't go to school. He has no social life. He lays in bed, in a

My husband is an episodic cluster sufferer.  Started in 1995 and came on hard and fast.  "Suicide headache" is about the only appropriate term for what he would suffer.  (And I loved the GP whom I tried to explain this to who suggested my husband might need to be checked into a facility if he was throwing around terms like suicide.)  My husband would get headaches every 6 or 12 or 18 months and the doctors didn't diagnose it for years.  It was a TERRIBLE time for him.
It took years before we got the cluster diagnosis and finally found Prednisone which ... kind of ... helped.  The steroid made him crazy.  Literally.  Aggressive, impatient, angry and mad which are polar opposites of his character.  Also, many times, the clusters would 'break through' when he weaned down to 10mg (after starting at 70 or 80!) and he would have to start the prednisone all over again.
10 years ago he found oxygen and that was a godsend.  Together with Imitrex and handfuls of whatever other pills were in the house, I could keep him vaguely able to handle the intense amount of pain that gripped him.  It was especially fun when our son was born and he was hit with the worst cluster ever (Spring 2003) and couldn't tolerate any noise at all.
We continued at this pace until 2008 when he was hit in March.  Prednisone allowed breakthroughs FOUR times and he was still having headaches in August.  The doctors (GP and Neuro) kept prescribing ever increasing doses of Prednisone - together with Topamax (migraines) and Verapamil (tension headaches) which had never done him any good.  One night when I was about at the end of my tether, I found this website - and it changed everything.

I've had CHs since 2009, and it wasn't until 2019 that I received any real form of treatment at all. It wasn't from lack of trying, but you know how doctors can be sometimes... At least I've been fortunate enough that some cycles skipped a year. I moved to Norway in 2017, and my luck turned around: my partner's father has worked for decades at an industrial/medicinal gas company. Take that, trepidatious healthcare system...
In June—almost 3 months ago—I started another cycle. For 2 months, it was fairly tame. On average I had one hit every 2 or 3 days, first thing in the morning, and I could nip it in the bud with oxygen. Completely manageable. So manageable that I decided to simply
not
bother with busting...

not
be able to get any. New COVID-19 regulations - all medical gas must be signed and accounted for within the system. He apologized and returned my tank - it ran out 10 minutes through my next hit.

I'm new to long CH (have had 2-3 day attacks about once a year, for maybe 5 or 6 years, and didn't even know they were CH just assumed they were bad migraines), but have had chronic migraine 22+ years. i have in-home and portable oxygen now, effective about 75% of the time. Also have suma injectors and 100mg pills (I'll take a half, so 50mg, grind it up and take it as a shot with energy drink if I'm out of injectors).
A friend came through out of the blue with some LSD paper blotters for me on Saturday. (Two days ago, it's now monday). I've tried to recreationally trip using blotters and mushrooms in the past with a 0% success rate. In the last 6 weeks I've learned a lot about busting with these tools so I've begun an attempt and I'd like your feedback (sorry for the long preamble)
ok, so I was 2 days (over 48hr but less than 72hrs) from last dose suma (was Injector, so 6mg). After much research I'm aware of two schools of throught regarding the interaction between suma and LSD - either a full blocking mechanism, or a tolerance related mechanism due to the similarity of the compounds. Not knowing which is which, I decided to hope for the best and just heavy dose.
4 tabs of approx 100ug paper. The produced effects were very slight, somewhere between 1-2 on the scale provided in the documents section of this website. sunday I remained pain free in total. I even was able to take about a 3 hour nap and awoke pain free. This is the first time in 7 weeks either of those things happened let alone on the same day. Hi all,
Long time lurker and first time post on a thread, ever, anywhere. So no clue if I'm posting in the right spot. Also, I have a lot of info that I want to share but I'll try not to ramble. I'm hoping that my story will be of some use to someone or perhaps someone smarter than me will be able to connect some dots perhaps.
About me: migraines starting out about 15 years old. First cluster at 19. Migraines stopped when clusters started. I'm almost 30 now (yikes) so I've been at this for awhile. Finally actually diagnosed with clusters at ~23 after going through the whole "we will diagnosis you with everything else first" thing that Drs seem to enjoy, even though the first time I read about a cluster headache, I knew that was the glass slipper (unfortunately). My clusters switch sides, about 2 weeks on each side with a couple days of rest between sides, and average about 9 months between cycles. And I'm female. So between the gender and side switching being rare for clusters, I'm guessing that added to the confusion with diagnosis.
Some things I have tried: all the triptans, ergotamine/cafergot, imitrex, relpax, zomig, OTC migraine everything (head on, excedrin, Tylenol, etc etc etc), lidocaine nasal spray, capsaicin in the nose, high flow oxygen, wisdom teeth removal, exercise, sleeping upright, sleeping in freezing cold, not sleeping, caffeine, chugging water, melatonin, quit smoking, smoking weed, MM, oxy and hydro, Xanax....I'm sure I'm missing some. Really, during a cluster I'll try anything.
None of these helped me except the MM, which I was super excited to finally try after reading about online (probably here) and finally finding some. I had high hopes that it would kill my cluster, which it did not. I ended up having to take them every night which had about a 90% success rate of keeping me from waking up with one. However, 30ish days of a daily dose adds up quick, supplies are limited, and it was disappointing that they didn't live up to the hype when I was sure they would be my saving grace. Also, not my favorite "vacation" if you know what I'm saying, lol.
To the unusual part: I know of someone who has an unlimited supply of humatrope, aka HGH, somatropin. It is an actual legit rx so I know it's nothing funky, seeing as I would be weary of injecting anything off the black market. Another friend had just started buying it as a supplement for his work out/bodybuilding regiment. As I said before, I'll take anything when I have a cluster and, maybe my friends are strange (quite possible lol), but they often have suggestions or random prescriptions (mostly migraine and pain killers) that they want to give me. People love to try and help. Well, I'm sitting around with some friends and a cluster starts, panic immediately sets in, and my hate for life is quickly escalating. We had just been talking about the HGH so someone googled and found an article that said someone's dr had suggested it. It wasn't an optimistic "definitely try this" kind

For example, there are thee ways to manage your weight gain weight, maintain the current weight y at or lose weight. A caloric surplus is when you take in more calories than you burn. A caloric maintenance is when you take in the number of calories that will keep you at your current weight and a caloric deficit is when you take in less calories than your bodies burn. You can find your caloric surplus maintenance and deficit by going online to and putting information about your age height weight and how much executes you do per week. That information you enter will then generate an estimate of what your caloric surplus, maintenance, and deficit will be. Nonetheless, here I am, back again just like times before. Feeling like a fool. As if the beast has just forgotten about me. So after 4 years, so blessedly long I almost forgot to worry about it, I had the first attack of my current cycle in early July.
In 23 years of ever increasingly random cycles (3 cycles in a year, two years off, one year with just one oddly long cycle, 4 years off, etc) I'd never tried the busting protocol before for a number of reasons. This time everything fell into place, and as the first attack was on a Saturday morning, I had all day Saturday to read up and prepare, and headed to the beach on Sunday with some friends to take a recreational size dose  of MM (for good measure) and take my first swing at busting,
And it....maybe worked? My attacks over the next week were low intensity, fended off with caffeine or a very small SPUT, and I prepared for my second busting dose. After that second dose things just kept getting worse.  I kept on avoiding Rx treatments, got me an O2 tank, and counted days until my third dose.  The week since that third dose has been the worst I've ever experienced - and it wasn't Slapbacks. I had hoped and convinced myself it was for a few days, until I couldn't pretend anymore.  This was a full on 5-8 in a day, even 4 days later, can't leave the house, scream myself hoarse, cry myself unconscious during an attack because I haven't slept in 2 days, all too familiar cycle of agony, exhaustion, fear/anxiety, and back to agony, pace, scream, weep and repeat, horrorshow.
At some point it occurred to me that I had likely sabotaged my own bust because I hadn't gone off my Pristiq (I know, I know, but that drug changed my life, and I was hoping, particularly after that first seemingly effective bust dose, that maybe I would be the exception to that rule.) (I know, I know. Now.).
I stopped the Pristiq (50 mg ER) immediately (in consult with my Dr); my last dose was Friday AM. It's been a white knuckle 5 days but I figured that late night Wednesday would be within the margin of error for 5 days.
So after 3 busting doses, I'm starting over tonight and making a renewed attempt (1.5g), with all "door closers" out of the picture.
I was feeling oddly optimistic - the bastard even showed up
as I was about to take the MM
as if to protest, and my usual mental pleas for mercy turned into threats, and every exhale of O2 was "
I'm coming for you, you *******".  It ended, I took my MM and settled into to watch some TV, and all was good until about 2 hours later, while I could still feel the effects of the MM, I got hit. Hard.
Has anyone else experienced a hit while in the middle of an MM busting dose? I recall having seen someone mention this when I was reading the forums for tips, but can't find that post now.  I know that none of this is exact science, and everyone has had different experiences, but this has me a bit worried - if I can get hit while my system is literally coursing with MM, does that mean my system wasn't fully clear and the door is still shut? Or, worse, that MM might now work for me?
